items.headline: Auto Sales Use Proposed at El Camino Real Commercial Site
items.summary: The Planning Commission considered a special development permit to allow auto sales at 975 W. El Camino Real, an existing commercial property in the ECR-C zone. The project involves site modifications including repaving, restriping, and landscape upgrades, but no building expansion or exterior changes.

items.headline: 195-unit apartment complex permitted near Berryessa transit station
items.summary: A $32 million multi-family development at 825 Berryessa Station Way is cleared to build, adding significant housing near the VTA light rail station. The project represents one of the larger residential approvals in the recent permit batch.

items.headline: Financial Assistance Pilot Program Launched for Water Customers
items.summary: San José City Council approved a new pilot program to provide financial assistance to water customers. The program aims to help residents manage water bill costs, though the specific eligibility criteria and funding amount were not detailed in the agenda.

items.headline: City faces potential litigation over Cuesta Park water main incident
items.summary: Mountain View's legal counsel is preparing for potential litigation related to a water main incident at Cuesta Park. The city is also negotiating real property matters involving two adjacent parcels on Clyde Avenue.

items.headline: Planning Commission to consider 27-unit townhome project with affordable units
items.summary: The Planning Commission will review a residential development featuring 27 three-story townhomes, including 5 affordable units. The project represents new housing in Cupertino with a significant affordable component.

items.headline: Council lawsuit filed by Mark Fantozzi against city and SummerHill Homes
items.summary: A litigation case (Mark Fantozzi v. City of Cupertino and SummerHill Homes, LLC, Case No. 26CV492787) is under legal discussion in closed session. Details of the dispute were not disclosed in the public agenda.

items.headline: 72-Unit Affordable Housing Project Proposed for Downtown Palo Alto
items.summary: City Council held a public hearing on a six-story, 100% affordable housing development at 450 Lytton Avenue that would replace a city-owned parking lot with 72 residential units. The project is requesting density bonus concessions under state law to make the development feasible on a small 0.43-acre parcel.

items.headline: Campbell adopts policy restricting use of city property for federal civil enforcement
items.summary: The City Council adopted a resolution establishing a policy that prohibits city-owned and city-operated property from being used for federal civil enforcement actions. This is a notable policy stance on how municipal resources can be deployed.
